text: Welcome to Fragmented, a podcast where we capture what's top of mind for Android developers.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I'm your host, Kaushik.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Today, we're talking about a developer's unsung hero, logging.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Logging is one of those incredibly useful tools that we often take for granted.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But not today.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Not here on Fragmented.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: We're diving deep into logging, and more specifically, a library you might not have heard of yet.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But first, let's take a step back and appreciate the reigning champion in Android logging, Timber.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If you're an Android developer, you've likely heard of Timber.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's the gold standard of logging and is a logging library created by Jake Wharton.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And it's become so popular that it's almost synonymous with logging in Android development.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But why use Timber?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Why not just rely on println or printline in Kotlin?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Oh, and there's another quick trivia.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Have you ever wondered what happens when you print line in your Android app?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: The Kotlin print line internally redirects to the well-known system.out.print line, which in any Java program prints to the standard output stream.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But here's the interesting part.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: In most emulators and modern devices today, that print line output stream redirects to your logcat in Android.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And it typically uses the info level priority of your log.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Pretty cool, huh?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I mean, at least I didn't know that.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Anyways, the other reason you don't want to be messing around too much with PrintLine directly in your Android app is that in order to write to your logcat file and console, you effectively are doing a bunch of IO operations to make that happen.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So just keep that in mind if you're, you know, really using a lens and eyeing the performance of your app.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Okay, let's go back to Timber.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: How does Timber help over PrintLine?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Well, there's three really nice benefits that Timber brings.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: The first, automatic tagging.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Timber automatically figures out which class you're logging from.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And in the tag portion of your log, it uses that class.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So if you ever log anything in your app, you'll know exactly which class that log came from.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Second, it has a nice and sleek API for string substitution and avoids you having to do some crazy concatenations.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: We'll come back to that one in some time, but at least for the time Timber was built with this really nice API for you to do string substitution and just have really nicely formatted logs.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: The third is probably the most important benefit in my opinion, and that's composite logging.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You can plant trees, different trees, and send your logs to these different trees, which in turn sends it to different places depending on the context.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: This, if you haven't figured out it also, where the inspiration for the name timber came from.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So with this composite logging, you can have, say, a regular tree that just dumps everything locally into Logcat.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That could be just one tree on the side.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You can have another one, a separate tree that says any log above a certain priority, like, say, warning and above.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Send that to Crashlytics, Sentry, Instabug, or one of these other Crash-based SDKs.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If you have a remote logging service, you know, many companies pay a lot of money for this service that is incredibly useful called Datadog.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You could have a separate Datadog tree and have all your logs remotely sent to this Datadog service, which you can then search and filter into a whole bunch of things.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You can effortlessly redirect any of your logs and do it all with Timber.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And that's where it really shines.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Personally, I love Timber's flexibility.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: For unit tests, for example, I inject a custom logger tree that simply just uses print line again.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's a clean and simple setup for my test log.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So when I run my unit tests...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Within the console with the results of my unit test, it also shows the output.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's kind of a nice setup.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I have a screenshot of this that you can look at in the show notes.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But here's where things get a little interesting.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: In one of my recent projects, I noticed some performance hits due to how we were logging.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: To be fair, this was largely our fault.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: We were logging massive serialized objects without too much thought into optimization, and we were logging a lot.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But it got me thinking, when was the last time someone spent time actually thinking about such a workhorse library or tool like Timber?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Are there any alternatives to Timber?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: How does Timber do their logging internally, and has someone thought about using a different approach, for example?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Turns out, sure enough, there are alternatives.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: There's one that I thought was particularly a fresh take on logging too, and it's called Logcat.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Yes, it's named after the very same Logcat we all know from Android Studio.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Finally confusing, but Logcat, the library...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: offers some really interesting improvements over Timber.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Now, I could go rambling on about it, but I thought, wouldn't it be better if I just bring the maintainer of the library to talk about it on the show?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And lucky for me and you, Logcat was built by my good friend and past guest of the show, PY of Square.
speaker: SPEAKER_01
